worth_it_explanation: Yield groups are how outside demand reaches the unified auction, so a thin or mistuned setup leaves money on the table on every impression that clears. The audit is quick to run because the partner list, status, and reporting all live inside Google Ad Manager, with no partner integration to stand up first. A partner that never bids, a timeout that drops slow bids, or a reporting gap that hides a money-losing partner each reduce net yield in a way the publisher can correct through configuration rather than a rebuild.
intro: Yield groups are how Google Ad Manager (GAM) brings third-party exchanges and networks into its unified auction through Open Bidding, so their setup decides how much outside demand pressure the publisher captures on each impression. A yield group names the inventory to open up, then lists the partners that compete in real time alongside Authorized Buyers and line items. These checks confirm that the intended partners are active and competing, that the bidder timeout balances bid capture against latency, and that partner revenue reconciles against the partners' own reports.

audit_checks.title: Yield group partners are not all competing in the unified auction
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A partner that was never added, or is added but inactive, does not bid, so the auction runs with less competition and clears at lower prices. The yield group looks populated, so the missing pressure on the auction goes unnoticed while the publisher earns less than the demand would support.

educate.base:
A yield group in Google Ad Manager (GAM) opens a slice of inventory to Open Bidding, where third-party exchanges and networks return bids over a server-to-server connection. Those partners compete in real time inside the same unified auction as Authorized Buyers and the publisher's own line items, and the highest net bid wins. Each partner you intend to use has to be added to the yield group and set to active for the format and environment it supports, or it never enters the auction.

When an intended partner is missing or inactive, the auction runs with fewer competing bids. Fewer bids mean less pressure on the clearing price, so impressions settle lower than the demand would otherwise support. The yield group still serves ads, so the shortfall does not announce itself. The revenue is simply lower than a fuller partner roster would produce.

A partner can also be present but unable to bid. A partner whose Ad Manager company profile is not activated for Open Bidding, or whose format and environment do not match the yield group's settings, will not appear or will not respond. Google's bid health view separates eligible requests from actual bids, which is where a partner that is configured but silent shows up.

A few signs point to partners not competing as intended:
  • A partner you expected to see is absent from the yield group's partner list
  • A listed partner is set to inactive rather than active
  • A partner is active but its bid health shows few or no bids against eligible requests
  • A partner's supported format or environment does not match the yield group's settings
You may want to confirm the intended partners are present, active, and actually bidding per format before treating the yield group as complete. A name in the list is not the same as a partner in the auction.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site running display and video, the intended partner roster often differs by format, so a partner active for display may be missing from the video yield group. You may want to confirm each format's yield group lists and activates the partners you negotiated for that format, rather than assuming one roster covers all of them.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, shopping and retail demand partners may be the ones you most want competing on product and review pages. You may want to confirm those partners are active in the yield groups covering that inventory. A retail partner that is absent there leaves the highest-intent pages with thinner competition.

investigate.intro: Confirm that each intended partner is present in the yield group, set to active, and actually returning bids for the formats it supports.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: Open 'Delivery', then 'Yield groups', and select a yield group you want to confirm.
Review the list of yield partners and note, for each, whether it is set to active or inactive and which format and environment it targets.
Compare the active partner list against the partners you intended to run for that format. Note any partner that is absent or inactive.
Open the Open Bidding bid health or reporting view and check, per partner, whether eligible requests are producing bids. Note any active partner that returns few or no bids.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per yield group, the intended partner roster and, for each partner, whether it is present, active, and returning bids against eligible requests. A yield group whose intended partners are all present, active, and bidding for the formats they support passes. A yield group missing an intended partner, holding a partner set to inactive, or holding an active partner that returns no bids is a fail recorded with the yield group name and the partner at fault.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the intended partner roster and each partner's status and bid activity for every yield group above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that not all of the intended Open Bidding partners are competing in {{site_name}}'s yield groups. Some partners are missing or set to inactive, and at least one active partner is returning few or no bids against eligible requests, so the unified auction runs with less competition and clears at lower prices than the demand would support. {{voice_recommend}} adding the missing partners, setting the intended partners to active for the formats they support, and confirming in the bid health view that each partner is actually returning bids. After the change, monitor the yield group's partner-level bids to confirm the added competition is reaching the auction.

audit_checks.title: Yield group bidder timeouts are too tight or too loose
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A timeout set too short drops the bids of slower partners before they arrive, which removes their competition and lowers the clearing price. A timeout set too long delays the auction and slows the page, which hurts viewability and the user experience. Either way the setting trades revenue against latency, and a mistuned value gives up one without buying enough of the other.

educate.base:
In Open Bidding, Google Ad Manager (GAM) sends bid requests to the yield group's partners over a server-to-server connection and waits a set window for their responses before resolving the unified auction. The bidder timeout is that window. It decides how long the auction holds open for partner bids to arrive, which is a direct trade between capturing more bids and keeping the auction fast.

A timeout set too short cuts off partners whose responses arrive a little slower. Their bids never enter the auction, so the competition thins and impressions clear lower than they would with those bids present. The lost revenue is invisible in the yield group itself because the auction still resolves and serves an ad. The only sign is in the bid health data, where a partner shows responses arriving after the window closed.

A timeout set too long carries the opposite cost. Because Open Bidding runs server-side, its waiting window adds to the time before the ad renders. Holding the auction open longer than the slow partners actually need delays the ad and the page, which can hurt viewability and the experience, especially on slower connections. The aim is a window wide enough to capture most partner bids and no wider.

The way to tune it is to read partner bid-response times against the current window. You may want to widen the timeout if capable partners are consistently responding just after it closes, and tighten it if the window sits well beyond when the partners finish bidding. Because the right value depends on the partner mix and the audience's connection profile, the setting is worth revisiting when the partner roster or the traffic changes rather than setting it once.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a content site where viewability and time to first ad matter for both revenue and reader experience, a long timeout that adds latency can cost more than the late bids it captures. You may want to weigh the partner bids that arrive late against the latency the wider window adds, and lean toward the window that captures most bids without a noticeable delay.

investigate.intro: Review the yield group's bidder timeout against the partners' actual bid-response times to judge whether the window captures most bids without an avoidable latency penalty.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: Open 'Delivery', then 'Yield groups', and select the yield group you want to review.
Note the bidder timeout configured for the yield group, if a timeout is exposed for your setup.
Open the Open Bidding bid health or reporting view and review, per partner, the bid-response timing and whether responses are arriving inside or after the window.
Note any capable partner whose bids consistently arrive just after the window closes, and note whether the window extends well beyond when partners finish responding.

google_publisher_console.label: Google Publisher Console
steps: Load a page that serves the yield group's inventory and open the Google Publisher Console from the page.
Review the ad request and delivery timing to see how long the auction holds before the ad renders.
Note whether the time to first ad looks driven by the Open Bidding window rather than by other page work.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the yield group's bidder timeout alongside the partners' observed bid-response times and the time to first ad. A timeout that captures most partner bids while keeping the ad render prompt passes. A timeout short enough that capable partners' bids arrive after it closes, or long enough that it delays the ad beyond when partners finish bidding, is a fail recorded with the yield group name and which side of the trade is off.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the bidder timeout, the partner bid-response times, and the time to first ad for each yield group above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that a yield group's bidder timeout on {{site_name}} is not tuned to the partners' actual bid-response times. The window is dropping the bids of slower partners before they arrive, or it is holding the auction open longer than the partners need and adding latency, so the setting is giving up revenue or speed without buying enough of the other. {{voice_recommend}} reading the partner bid-response times against the current window, then adjusting the timeout to capture most partner bids while keeping the ad render prompt. After the change, recheck the bid health timing and the time to first ad to confirm the new window holds the balance.

audit_checks.title: Partner CPMs in yield groups are not validated against partner reports
audit_checks.impact_rationale: An Open Bidding partner reports its own revenue and cost-per-mille (CPM), and a large gap between the partner's numbers and Google Ad Manager's points to a misconfigured partner, a real discrepancy, or a partner that nets negative after fees. Without a reconciliation the publisher cannot tell a healthy partner from one that is costing more than it returns, so a money-losing partner stays in the auction.

educate.base:
Each Open Bidding partner in a yield group reports its own revenue and CPM in its own dashboard, and Google Ad Manager reports a separate set of partner-level figures in yield group reporting. The two systems measure the same traffic from different sides, so some difference is expected. Google notes that reporting discrepancies are expected when multiple systems measure the same delivery, and that Open Bidding payments are calculated from Google Ad Manager reporting. A large or growing gap, though, is a signal worth tracing rather than accepting.

A big gap usually points to one of a few causes. The partner and Google Ad Manager may count on different time zones or with different methodologies, which inflates the gap without anything being wrong. Overlapping yield group targeting can double-count impressions across groups. Or the partner may genuinely be returning less than its reporting implies once its own fees are netted out, in which case the partner contributes little or even nets negative after costs.

The way to tell these apart is to reconcile the two reports per partner for the same period and the same time zone. Yield group reporting in Google Ad Manager exposes the 'Yield group buyer' dimension along with yield group estimated CPM and estimated revenue, which line up against the partner's own revenue and CPM. When the two reconcile within a reasonable tolerance, the partner is measuring as expected. When they diverge sharply, the partner setup, the targeting, or the partner's net contribution needs a closer look.

You may want to run this reconciliation for each active partner rather than only at the yield group total. A single partner with a misconfigured integration can hide inside a yield group whose overall numbers look reasonable. A partner that reconciles poorly and nets negative after fees is a candidate to fix or remove.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site running several partners across formats, the reconciliation is most useful per partner and per format because a partner can reconcile well on display and poorly on video. You may want to check each active partner against its own report for the formats it serves, rather than trusting a single blended yield group figure.

investigate.intro: Reconcile each Open Bidding partner's reported revenue and CPM against Google Ad Manager's yield group reporting for the same period and time zone.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: Open 'Reporting', then build or open a yield group report and add the 'Yield group buyer' dimension along with yield group estimated CPM and estimated revenue.
Set the report to a fixed period and note the time zone the report uses.
Record, per partner, the impressions, estimated CPM, and estimated revenue Google Ad Manager reports for that period.

partner_dashboard.label: Open Bidding partner dashboard
steps: Sign in to each active partner's own reporting dashboard.
Pull the same fixed period and the same time zone you used in Google Ad Manager.
Record the partner's reported revenue and CPM, and any net figure after the partner's fees, then compare it against the Google Ad Manager numbers for that partner.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per active partner, the Google Ad Manager estimated revenue and CPM and the partner's own reported revenue and CPM for the same period and time zone. A partner whose two reports reconcile within a reasonable tolerance and whose net contribution after fees is positive passes. A partner with a large unexplained gap, or one that nets negative after fees, is a fail recorded with the partner name and the size of the gap.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the Google Ad Manager and partner-reported revenue and CPM for each active partner above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the Open Bidding partner CPMs in {{site_name}}'s yield groups have not been reconciled against the partners' own reports. At least one partner shows a large gap between its reported revenue and CPM and what Google Ad Manager reports for the same period, which can point to a misconfigured integration, a time zone or targeting discrepancy, or a partner that nets negative after its fees. {{voice_recommend}} reconciling each active partner's report against the yield group reporting for the same period and time zone, then tracing or removing any partner that reconciles poorly or contributes negatively after fees. After the change, recheck the partner-level figures to confirm the reports agree within a reasonable tolerance.
